description: Wednesday 19 November 2025 | Standing Orders Committee Hearing of evidence | Review of Standing Orders 2026 Hon James Meager Wellington Community Justice Project Jordan Lawson Leon Caslavsky Dr Dean Knight New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Debra Angus Bryce Lyall Professor Jane Kelsey Ngā Toki Whakarururanga Jane Kelsey, Member

chunks.text: Gotta bring up his thing. Bring your hands. Uh, James, please. Um, Honorable James, give us good afternoon, Mr. Chair, colleagues. Very weird being down this end of the table, but I'll give him my best crack. He's my very first submission to a select committee, so, um, do treat gently. Uh, you've got my, your, my, um, WR submissions, so I won't reverse, uh, in, in too much detail.

There are, I think I raised, um, a, a few points just from my perspective, both as Chair of the Justice Committee, where we sat through quite, uh, an intense piece of legislation. Uh, I made that point, uh, on the, on the front page, um, around the purposes of the committee scrutiny. We've also made some comments in my submission around conduct of members during committees and expectations around attendance at select committees.

The, are sort of the three key points in the written submission. And I thought I'd raise, um, just some key comments on those, and then move on to some other thoughts I've had, having watched and read a number of other submissions just around the process for, um, scrutiny of legislation. I think we've come to a point, and I particularly experienced this during the principles of the Treaty Wang bill, um, where the, the volume of submissions on, um, you know, whether they are controversial pieces of legislation or, um, even quite standard bits

of legislation these days are quite significant. And that has had an impact in how I think select committees are able to deal with them and process them to the point where, I think I made a comment in the submission around, um, risking getting a, to a situation where you've got AI generated submissions being read by AI machines to provide summaries into a report which is drafted by ai.

And I don't know if that's a, that's a realistic outcome. It might be taking things to the extreme end, but certainly the volume of submissions that came through in that process. And then if you look at some of the other bills that have gone through, and just the sheer quantity of, um, very short, concise online submissions has made the administration of it quite difficult.

And I think that leads to the point where, um, my view of the select committee process is that it's supposed to be about scrutinizing the quality of the legislation, improving it as much as possible, pointing out unintended consequences, uh, rather than be a platform for, um, advocacy or your voice as to whether or not a policy is the right thing to do or not.

There is always obviously a room for that, but I, I fear that we go too far down the pathway of, um, treating it as a poll or a referendum and getting away from discerning what are some high quality submissions that are actually gonna impact and change the legislation to improve it as much as possible. Um, because of course, the, as legislators, we are there to try and get the best quality legislation regardless of whether or not we agree with the substance.

So that's the thrust of the first part of my submission. The second was around conduct at select committees and just the difficulty. Um, and, and I, I felt as chair that, um, I had with maintaining order sometimes given the tools that are available. I think on the whole, as a committee, we, we did a pretty good job and we're relatively collegial the whole time.

But I think you run into issues under the current rules when, and I I'm not gonna get into particular details or individuals, although I gave an example in my written submission when the normal political constraints on, uh, behavioral conduct, um, don't hold because the, the purpose is to provocate and to, um, create attention and to create disorder.

And whether that's, um, deliberate or not, the, the, the current controls really are political controls by saying that, well, um, if we point out this behavior, they'll reign themselves in because there'll be a political consequence when that is part of the reason for the behavior or the conduct. I think that doesn't hold, um, much strength anymore. So I wonder whether or not there is anything the committee needs to look at in terms of strengthening powers available to the chair.

I'm, I'm, I haven't made any suggestions as to what that might look like, but I think if you're, if you're, uh, in an environment where the performance is the purpose and you don't care about the consequences, it can become difficult. The third key point, I think, in my submission was just to generally around attendance through select committees.

And here I did make some recommendations as to what, um, standing orders, uh, review could look at. I, I feel like the main thing is to have some consistency across select committees as to what the expectations are around attendance. I feel like if we're going to, um, hold the, particularly the submissions process as being a, a significant part of our legislative process, I think it, it deserves a bit more, um, manner or respect beyond submitters turning up to an empty room and steering at TV screens.

I, I understand that is maybe a consequence of how we all operate these days, particularly if you're holding hearings and recess weeks. But I, I would feel this is submitted very disappointed if I turn up to a room. Yeah. And there was, um, um, you know, there was administrative staff and all the mps were on the screen, and so I think having some expectations around attendance, um, particularly during sitting weeks, particularly when you are in the building, I think you should have a very good reason if you are in the

building not to be attending. And so I've made some recommendations around that as well. And just finally having watched a number of submissions and, um, and read, uh, a good number of submissions too, I wanted to raise some wider comments just around some issues that have been raised. I contributed to the National Party submission, and I made some, um, suggestions around the, the, the hours of parliament.

And so my, if, if you get a chance to look at the National Party submission, my views were around, um, essentially running recess weeks and Parliament weeks, um, extending the length of time you sit during a week, increasing the amount of time we have for scrutiny and, and for legislation to eventually reduce the amount of urgency that's used, but also create a, a clear distinction between, um, time you can spend out of Parliament in the electorate and the time you spend in Parliament.

I was in Europe visiting the European, uh, commission and European Parliament, and they have what they call Strassburg weeks, which is, um, every so often there will be a sitting in Strassburg. And essentially it is, um, the, the entire population of, um, parliament in Brussels essentially moves to Strasbourg for a week, and that is where they spend their time.

And I think that's not a bad principle saying, um, if you've got a sitting week, it is a sitting week, and if you've got a recess week, it is a recess week. And you can distinguish between the both. And the proposal I put forward, um, what's in the National Party submission, um, could result in both more time to legislate, but also more time at home and less disruption for schedules.

So I think that's worth looking at. It also, uh, recommends maybe reducing the hours you spend legislating into the night so that both staff and mps can have somewhat more of a normal, um, work life balance. Um, I think it's difficult to get quality representation when, um, you are working through to 10, 10 30 at night, and then you're up at 5 36.

Um, trying to, to add value to a select committee, uh, when you've been pretty much going nonstop. I think there, there's a bit of balance that could be reached there. And just with time running out, I want to make a couple comments just on some minor issues. Um, one was around legislative statements. Um, I understand the purpose of them. I'm not sure they've achieved, um, what they were set out to achieve.

The purpose was to provide legislative statement at the introduction, or first reading, sorry, of a bill and second reading so that, um, ministers essentially didn't even read out their first or second reading speeches. I don't think it's achieved that. And I wonder whether or not there is any value in continuing on with them. Um, if all they are is providing a one page summary of the bill for a minister to then read out their speech, I'm not sure if it's, it's worth keeping on.

Um, I made some points around, um, general debate and special debates that have been raised. I, I, I think the purpose of parliament is to legislate and there are other platforms with which you can, um, make political speeches. I think in it around question time, I made some comments around, um, probably not, probably not appreciated by whether or not you're in government.

If you're in government, the idea of having slightly, um, more intense scrutiny and less ability to promote government policies, um, might not fall favor when you're in government. But I think it's important that question time is for scrutiny of the executive. Um, so I've made some suggestions around there about how that might change, but particularly in the, in the National Party one.

Yeah, there's some, um, sort of summary comments around how you might change question time to put more scrutiny on the executive while still allowing members of the governing parties the ability to question 'cause they are, you know, mps in their own right. Um, the one thing I didn't put in there, which I think it would be worth raising, perhaps it was in there, was I actually think that question time would operate better if, um, if there was more. Sorry, No mps there.

Well, that would be entirely in the hands of the speaker. I think he's been incredibly restrained over the past wee while. Um, and look, I understand this back and back and forth between opposition on this, but if, if the discretion of whether or not a question was an order or an answer was given was entirely up to the speaker, and, um, if there was more supplementaries available, the opposition could, um, could point out a non-answer through repetitive questioning in the same, um, style, same substance for that non answerer to be repeated.

And then they can use that as an example of a minister not quite addressing or approaching the question rather than sometimes we get to a back and forth, um, where people are challenging essentially the rulings of the speaker. And it, it does disrupt the, the flow of question time, I think a little bit, but that's not the, the point I'm particularly, um, gonna die in a ditch over.

And then finally, just to comment on committee, the whole house stage, I actually think it works relatively well. I think you wouldn't need much change in the way of specific times for ministers to speak or limits or anything like that. I think what might help the flow and, um, the, the, the, um, I guess the, the frustrations that the opposition have on members day and that government members have on normal days with the dragging out of committee stages is if there's more focus on relevance and repetition, you might get better quality questions

and better quality debates, um, without, um, you know, having to put the presiding officer in a position of making calls over closing a debate quite early because someone's gone on for two or three minutes about some titles that are clearly frivolous. So I think you wouldn't need to change the committee the whole stage, but some more focus on relevance and repetition could sharpen up the questions and sharpen up the debate without diminishing the fact that, um, you know, if these are being held during urgency, there is more scope to be given.

I think more latitude to the presiding officer would be good. That's really the summary of it. Well, James, thank you. I think you, that's a very good submission that you've given yourself. You've raised a number of things that we should look at. I think you'll, um, appreciate that there are a number of things in your submission that were also mentioned last week. Yes. Uh, when you were here, um, observing just on the issue of, um, speaker discretion around, um, questions.

The questions are approved each day by the speaker and are knocked out, but not in order. Uh, and when it comes to supplementaries, the long standard practice, which we may need to look at to be more prescriptive on, is that if there is something that comes up in an answer, uh, that expands the, the scope of the question, then other supplementaries, uh, around that become, uh, an order that becomes frustrating for both, um, uh, both sides of the house at times I've noticed.

But, um, I do listen very intently just to try and pick those things up. And sometimes it's just one word, word that gets missed. Um, but that one word is a huge contextual effect. So, um, it's just me justifying my, No, and I, I think I actually, my my view on that is that I think a lot of the frustrations from the opposition comes from fact.

They've got limited numbers of, so I worked in opposition and we were responsible for allocating out supplementaries. And by the time you give a generous allocation to leader opposition, you know, as a, as a back bench opposition mp, you might have three or four subs that you can follow up The actual may, the actual, um, the actual, uh, allocation of question.

It's to you, well, it, it, um, under Jonathan Hunt, it changed to, um, I prescribed this kind of a formula. Um, and I I did make that statement to the house the other day that I would be treating it much more, uh, in line with standing orders, which, because pre speaker yeah, I haven't done that to any, I've only done that twice but far.

And, um, I personally think that there should be some agreement around that, uh, because the time doesn't necessarily belong to, uh, one side or the other. But it is, as you point out, a very valuable time for opposition. An important part of the accountability. I think, um, uh, you know, if you, if you look in the last parliament, the government of the day, uh, had six questions, if not seven every day, which was, um, you know, pretty frustrating for opposition. But, you know, And like, and likewise, when I worked in opposition, um,

I think the National Party at 56 mps at that point, so I had Yep. Had the vast majority of the questions in a plethora of supplementaries. I, I think what happens is that when you get limited with the, the amount of supplementaries you have been allocated or, or may or think you may have, you fight for every single one to make sure you get an answer or the question addressed out of everyone.

If there were more supplementaries or more time, perhaps you wouldn't be as, um, as agitating or, or looking to the Whipple leader of the house to try and get that question back. So you can ask another one. You can just keep pursuing with three or four or five. Yeah. I should point out when I first came to Parliament, um, uh, for then speaker to kid allowed two supplementaries from the person asking the question and up up to two from Opposition party.

Oh yeah, thank you. Can I call to the table? Sorry. It is the Wellington Community Justice. They here, they're, Yes, we're here via Zoom, If you can hear us. Thank you. Right in front of me. Well done. Um, yes. Well, thank you for giving us the opportunity to submit.

Um, the Wellington Community Justice Project is made up of law students, uh, from Victoria University of Wellington, who submit on bills, um, either from a sort of law students with far too much time on their hands perspective, or, um, if they think that the interest of community justice could, um, somehow be improved via, uh, some amendments, uh, to bills or, um, to standing orders.

And so we've written this submission, Jordan and I, um, where we essentially outline how we think, uh, that parliament could be improved if the speaker were given a power to, uh, be allowed to prevent the publication of, um, certain video clips from Parliament with a breach privilege.

Uh, on the basis that now that we're sort of in the internet age, uh, there's a risk that Parliament could become, uh, merely a, a platform for the expression of views to online audiences rather than for reason dialogue between members of Parliament. Um, and we think that while there are obviously penalties that can be imposed by the house upon its members where they breach privilege, um, the inclusion of this particular power would, um, sort of change the incentive structures so that, uh, there's more of a focus on, uh, engaging with the house, rather engage,

engaging with outside audiences. Jordan, did you have anything to add, Add, um, yeah, the, what the main, um, concern that I'm preempting from the, um, committee today is that obviously we've got Parliament, t tv and you know, this, uh, session right now is being live streamed and anyone can access it at any time.

And that's the point of open democracy. And we're not suggesting that be limited in any way. Um, the only, the, what we are suggesting is that, uh, particular mps considering they have, they tend to have a, a platform, a social media platform, and in some cases a very large, um, a way to generate content is through the activities of the house and what they can do in the house.

Um, and so we would suggest that where there has been a clear breach of privilege, um, and the SP speaker sees a fit that, um, we say, okay, the individual member of Parliament should be limited from reaping the benefits of that, of that breach and the media and Parliament TV in its unfiltered capacity.

And the media, and it's the traditional media in its filtered capacity should be left to, um, share that as, as it sees fit. Um, yeah, that's what I had to add there. If you wanna continue, Leo, I think you, oh, sorry. You, you raise a a very good point. Something that we're all very mindful of. Um, I would point out that the speaker at the moment can ask, uh, people who put post, uh, material that misrepresents what's said in Parliament, uh, uh, to take it down.

And we've had instances of that, and there's been cooperation on that. And I think it's a matter of people understanding what the bounds are, what they can and can't do. Uh, but it, we've gotta be very careful that we don't, uh, uh, uh, and I suppose you'd say block modern communications that, uh, people are using, people are accessing news in very different ways these days.

So it's something that we will certainly give some thought to. Uh, 'cause it is, it is a, I suppose it's the per performative aspect of, uh, social media that, uh, uh, we are really talking about. I, I, I think it is a valid concern about, um, limiting publication.

And, you know, you want, you want audiences to engage with parliament, and we, and we note that concern in our submission, but we, but we also think that, I mean, there, there's, there's often talk about a lack of trust in institutions, uh, and part particularly in in Parliament sometimes. And I think that, um, if, if Parliament were to limit the publication of, you know, particularly, uh, of, of instances which are, which are particularly sort of disrespectful towards the house, um, then I think that would filter out, filter out some things that,

you know, the house considers shouldn't be happening and, um, wouldn't allow MPS to sort of gain engagement from that and, and profit off of it, you know? And so it could be reported, but perhaps in a more informative and neutral manner rather than a look at me go, I'm, I'm, I'm just reflecting the house and that that appeals to my particular segment of, of the audience.

Um, I think that would be a sufficient protection. Yeah. Look, it's a very live issue, uh, one that we are quite awake to, and it is useful to have your submission, um, uh, reinforcing a view that, uh, that we need to look at it. So thank you very much for your time this afternoon. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

To committee, um, Dean Knight, professor of Law, te aka Victoria University of Wellington, very brief submission. Two points, and as per my written submission, urgency in the composition of voting select committees, but also like to add something very briefly that's arisen from your deliberation, that's managing the scarce time for selectivity hearing of submissions, especially the role of academics and those people from knowledge institutions.

So, uh, but very brief on on each of those first urgency, I think it's fair to say urgency and expedited law making has become an addiction at the cost of good governance and checks and balances. And what is a delicate, uh, constitutional ecosystem? A constitutional ecosystem, which is a political constitution, which, um, has a lot of checks and balances exercised through the political process.

And we don't give time for that political process to, uh, to do its work. We don't get the checks and balances. So urgency expedition acts like a bonfire for the, on those checks and balances. And that really worries me. And I think, I think we can always take it as a given. It's, the community has, uh, been quite, um, activated by this.

And I don't wanna get into the pack drill of, of who's been using it for what and things like that. The question is how do we break it? And so I've made the suggestions about, uh, first the suggestion of, uh, tweaking the threshold for urgency and using some different language of requiring reasons for urgency, which are, I think it suggest detailed, sufficient and proportionate.

Um, and along with the speaker's ability to rule on the adequacy, which I think it applies to extra ordinary urgency, but would operate slightly differently if we had a slightly more elevated and precise, uh, threshold for urgency, particularly one that talks about proportionality. So it's not just a given. There is has gotta be something, as, you know, very special to, uh, uh, a reason for, um, urgency.

That's one thing. And, and, uh, but my hobby horse is actually posten enactment review, because I think that potentially takes away much of the political advantage of urgency and expedition restores. The cost of the lost, um, uh, restores the lost public participation that would've had to happen in select committees if it had gone through the, um, extra process and ensures the value of the select committee scrutiny is, is, is preserved.

And we saw it used, uh, in the, uh, with the government's consent with the COVID-19 Public Health Response Act. A number of academics made that call and it was picked up and it was the right thing to do. The, the question is whether we can make that obligatory for situations where urgency has been used, where, uh, select committee scrutiny has been, uh, circumvented or been done under such time pressure that it is, uh, in, in inadequate.

So I'm a big fan of that. I think you can see the advantages of, of keeping the political, uh, conversations going to ensure that the government, uh, is exposed to the accountability for those legislative measures. The one p uh, postscript ad from the COVID-19 experience is the product of the Posten enactment review needs to be in the live and back in the, um, parliamentary and political process, difficulty with the co COVID-19 one, uh, recommendations was they came out three months later and they just gathered dust.

So my suggestion is that if you're doing this, the, the recommendations as lead committee can somehow come back in as if it was a second reading or something like that. So that the, the, the government has to wear the political cost of, of, of, of dealing with that swiftly. And, and, and the idea being that if there's really constructive select committee comment, it would be in the government's advantage to continue that, that vehicle through to basically pick up what would otherwise be lost.

So that's my urgency suggestion. Um, select committee compensate, uh, composition and voting here. I'm trying to square the circle between, I think the two functions of select committee, both legislative scrutiny and executive scrutiny. And as it relates to legislative scrutiny, I think we can expect relatively constructive pass passage of government initiatives in situations where select committees don't act as just wrecking balls for legislative, um, uh, initiatives to require that to be reinstated in the committee of the whole,

which I don't think is in anybody's benefit. We want to foster that, that nonpartisan constructive improvement of bills. And so, uh, that legisl scrutiny, how do we, how do we predict and preserve that a go majority in those committees is understandable as it relates to executive scrutiny. I think there's more scrutiny for, say, scope for saying that process should be opposition lead, and a situation where you might say that those committees should have a, um, composition which reflects the proportions of the non-executive members of the house.

So that's my proposition that the two have different needs for different sort of compositions. The capacity in a small house with very few mps means we can't have separate committees for those, both of those things. Um, which I think is the practice in some overseas, uh, jurisdiction. I'm not a comparativist on this, but, but that's a poss that was, that's how it's been dealt with elsewhere.

But I've raised the question of whether we can actually square that circle with allowing party voting and select committees based on different compositions, based on the particular function that these select committees engaged in cast by a senior mp. Not sure quite on the practicalities, but I think that's one way to try and get a pragmatic way through it. But I think the practicalities are, are serious because there's 12 select committees.

Uh, we struggle to get enough people to, uh, fill 'em all. Uh, the proportion of select committees is proportionate to parliament by and large mm-hmm. With a number, uh, being, uh, balanced select committees, half opposition, half mm-hmm. Uh, government. So I, I just don't think that that's a great idea.

And we all, we're all for having greater, you know, ability to do things. What I would worry about that situation would be that you could end up with, uh, bills being returned to the house unaltered because of the, the, um, an ability to get a, a, a vote one way or the other.

And that, um, 'cause at the moment there, there are some select committees where it takes opposition votes, uh, to get it back into parliament. And I think, um, uh, I think we've just gotta be careful that we don't actually end up with a process that totally disrespects any submissions to Bill at all.

And I'm, I think I'm trying to address that by saying, I think the way to do it is the waitings, rather than being reflected just in the personnel who who are on the committee, are actually carried by them as as a card and the, and the for legislative matters, the ordinary, uh, um, uh, proportions of the house would apply to avoid that, that situation of, of Stonewall and, and those sorts of things.

But when it relates to scrutiny, you've gotta different composition because it's slightly different. I mean, I'm, I'm happy just, uh, oh, no, We, we'll certainly have a look at that. I have a quibble as an academic in a more general suggestion, um, because the formal practices develop that if you're an individual submitter, you get five minutes, you're a organization, one, you get 10 minutes.

I think the language is actually, it is five minutes of a personal submission. Uh, the difficulty is for, for, for academics from knowledge institutions, we fall between the two. Because when I make a submission, I don't make a submission personally. I make a submission in the course of my employment as part of my, the university's critical and conscience function.

Um, but because of academic freedom, the views that I express are my own and not of my employer or my colleagues. So practically what that means is when we submit our, make our submissions like this one here, I've submitted as Dr. Dean Knight, but it really should be Dr. Dean Knight of Victoria, university of Wellington. And what that means, the cost of that is that means that, uh, academics have only been getting five minutes on, on some bills when organizations get 10 minutes, been suggested to many people just invent an organization.

I'm not prepared to go there. 'cause I, you know, because I think, I think, I think we take seriously the role that academics and, and, and those from, um, knowledge institutions may provide to that. And I say that as knowledge institution, universities have a statutory mandate, uh, to be critical and conscience of society. So we're actually designed to feed into process like that.

And I don't want to privilege our particular views over others, but I think it's Talk to, we are very aware of this problem. Yeah. And, um, uh, it's, it's one that's grown over a number of parliaments and the, the, one of the problems is the volume of submissions that you now get and the inability in outstanding orders to do the sort of triage work that we should do, uh, to make sure that there is, uh, uh, that those who have expertise that are valuable can be a bit more.

Um, the society's view is that while, uh, standing order 2 6 9 has provided a process through which the Attorney General can signal inconsistencies to Parliament, um, it's been largely ine in taking that next step, which would be, uh, acting as a limit on introduction or passing of bills, which are inconsistent with the Bill of Rights Act, or providing a real prompt to Parliament to address inconsistencies during the legislative process.

Um, so the law society wants to be, uh, productive and, and look at ways in which, uh, that might be addressed. Um, and I think the guiding principle for the Law Society is that, um, any decision to legislate in a way that infringes on the protected rights must be taken after full consideration of the Attorney General's advice and as a deliberate decision of the house, rather than the advice, um, being perhaps a, a box ticked in the process.

Um, there's two ways that the Law Society has suggested, um, that these standard orders could be amended to bolster this process. The first is a paragraph 2.28, um, and that's the, uh, responsible minister providing the relevant select committee, um, the purpose, uh, or justification for enacting the infringing provision.

Um, so I think the, the key things that the law society wishes to see or would recommend is that, first of all, um, there should be a necessity test before AI is used at the moment. Um, so the committee should come to the view that it's ne necessary given, um, inadequate time or resourcing.

The second issue, uh, which the chair will be very familiar with from previous submissions, both to standing orders and Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Committee, uh, is the International Treaty Examination process. Now, I have spent four decades working on these agreements.

The peremptory process of the select committees is worst, I think with foreign affairs, defense and trade for several reasons. But in particular because it's dealing with treaties that have not been through a parliamentary process. So it's not had that kind of scrutiny in the development of the legislation.

So when the select committee gets the treaty, it is effectively a done deal. We have the secrecy that surrounds the process unless the leaks. So there's not been an ability of people, even myself, who has a lot of expertise in this to prepare a submission that goes through a 20 to 30 chapter arcane international agree.

And when I'm dealing with trade and investment agreements, really complex stuff, select committees do not have that expertise. They do not understand those agreements. And so you need to have effective submissions that can deconstruct them.

It's not enough to deal with implementing legislation, because that's after the fact. Implementing legislation often doesn't deal with many of the key issues in those agreements. Um, and the government, um, has a majority get them through anyway. The biggest problem on top of that is that the committee becomes captive of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade.

The national interest analysis that MFA does is not independent. It's actually a justification for what they have negotiated. And it is not subject to any form of, of robust independent critique. This is a process that has been critiqued since the mid 1990s, and in paragraph 38 of my submission, I list a whole series of those going back to the Clark of the House David McGee back in 1996. These have consistently been ignored because it doesn't suit governments to have what happens in these treaty subject to scrutiny, and it doesn't suit the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and trade to be accountable to anybody.

There was a trade for all advisory group that made a report that, uh, New Zealand should adopt a system similar to the Australian in, uh, joint Standing Committee on treaties. That's has expertise, has independence, is able to secure external analysis, um, and produces relatively robust, um, reports.

The chair of the committee said, well, we don't need to conduct a review then. And so this is hugely frustrating, right? Mm.
